topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the room had become a thick, suffocating thing. I was meticulously organizing my desk, a task I usually found soothing, but today the movements felt jerky, hesitant. I glanced over at Liam, who was ostensibly studying, his head bent low over a thick, leather-bound book. My eyes drifted to the page. It wasn't just open; it was clearly marked, a specific passage underlined with a faint pencil smudge. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I cleared my throat, a dry, rasping sound in the quiet. "Hey, Liam," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally high. He flinched, a quick, almost imperceptible jerk of his shoulders, and snapped the book shut with unnecessary force. He looked up, his expression carefully neutral, but his eyes flickered away from mine too quickly. I stood up abruptly, needing distance. The air felt too thin in my lungs.

[story 2]

It started subtly. I noticed my diary, which I kept locked in my drawer, was slightly ajar this morning. When I opened it, the entries were not mine. The handwriting was familiar—Liam’s looping script—but the contents detailed events I had never experienced, anxieties I hadn't voiced. My hands started shaking as I reread a recent entry describing a late-night confrontation that I distinctly remembered having with a different person. I kept glancing towards the doorway, half-expecting him to walk in, a ghost materializing from the shadows. I walked over to his side of the room, my steps slow and deliberate, and pointed a trembling finger at the open journal lying on his nightstand. "What is this?" I asked, the words coming out strained and brittle. He didn't meet my gaze; instead, he picked up the book, his fingers brushing against the paper as if it were something delicate and fragile, and offered a mumbled, dismissive reply.

[story 3]

I found myself watching him from across the room, an involuntary habit that made my jaw ache. He was hunched over his laptop, but I could see the way his gaze kept drifting toward the corner of his desk where my personal notebook lay. The atmosphere was charged, heavy with unspoken things. When he finally looked up, catching my stare, there was a distinct hesitation in his expression, a momentary blankness before he quickly refocused on his screen. I took a deep breath, trying to keep my tone level, but I could feel the tension coiling in my chest, a tight, restless energy. "Liam," I began, my voice carefully modulated, "have you been looking at my journal?" The silence that followed stretched taut, filled only by the low hum of the ventilation system. He slowly lowered the laptop, his posture shifting from relaxed to guarded, and for a long moment, he just stared at the floor, avoiding any acknowledgment of the accusation hanging between us.
